sections.heading: Structure and meaning
sections.body: If + past simple, would + infinitive. The second conditional describes unreal or unlikely situations in the present or future. The past tense in the if-clause doesn't refer to the past — it creates 'distance' from reality.

sections.heading: Were, not was
sections.body: In formal English and in careful speech, use 'were' for all persons in second conditional if-clauses: 'If I were you...', 'If she were here...', 'If it were possible...'. 'Was' is increasingly accepted informally, but 'were' is the safe choice.

sections.heading: Giving advice
sections.body: 'If I were you, I would...' is the standard structure for advice. It's extremely common and useful in both professional and personal contexts.

examples.english: If I had more time, I'd learn the piano.
examples.note: Unreal present — I don't have more time

examples.english: If she were taller, she could be a model.
If I were you, I'd apologize immediately.
What would you do if you won the lottery?

items: If I ___ (be) you, I ___ (tell) him the truth.
If he ___ (study) medicine, he ___ (become) a doctor.
What ___ you ___ (do) if you ___ (lose) your job?

quiz.question: The second conditional describes:
quiz.explanation: Second conditional = imaginary or unlikely present/future. The past form in the if-clause signals distance from reality.
